key_metrics_output: polymer type, chemical additives (dyes, pigments, coatings, finishing agents), size, morphology, chemical profile, developmental/endocrine/oxidative stress endpoints
abstract: This perspective article calls for increased chemical transparency and coordination in microfiber research. The authors highlight that microfibers (anthropogenic fibers < 5 mm) are widespread in air, water, and sediment with evidence of human exposure, but the chemical dimension of microfiber hazards remains largely uncharacterized. Textiles contain undisclosed dyes, pigments, coatings, and finishing agents that may be toxic, persistent, or bioaccumulative. The paper identifies three priority areas: (1) chemical prioritization using a tiered scheme (Tier A: high-priority additives; Tier B: contextual finishing aids; Tier C: exploratory compounds), (2) study harmonization including standardized characterization and environmentally relevant test conditions, and (3) collaboration infrastructure spanning academia, NGOs, and industry to enable data sharing, reference materials development, and translation of findings into safer product design.

particle_size_range: Microfibers (anthropogenic fibers < 5 mm, synthetic, semisynthetic, and natural)
key_metrics_output: textiles contain dyes, pigments, coatings, finishing agents, and unintentionally added chemicals
harmonizing microfiber characterization (e.g., size, morphology, polymer type, and chemical profile)
End points, many of which have already been explored but not codified, could include developmental, endocrine, oxidative stress, systemic biochemical disruption, or behavioral responses
abstract: Microfibers (anthropogenic fibers < 5 mm, synthetic, semisynthetic, and natural) are widespread across air, water, and sediment, with growing evidence of human exposure
the chemical dimension of microfiber hazards largely remains uncharacterized: textiles contain dyes, pigments, coatings, finishing agents, and unintentionally added chemicals
